storage_policy.refresh_policy: registered producers refresh after successful completion

entries.title: Historical Host MLIR AOT GEMM Benchmark
entries.claim_boundary: Host CPU AOT correctness and timing evidence from the earlier generic Transform pipeline; it is not the Chapter 3 frozen dataset and does not demonstrate BLIS replacement.

paper_use.decision: Use only to document the earlier generic Transform/AOT pipeline. Use Chapter 3 for frozen preliminary statistics.
usable_claims: The host MLIR Transform-to-AOT path compiled, ran, and checked correctness.

entries.title: Historical Native C++ Tile Sanity Benchmark
entries.claim_boundary: A native C++ schedule sanity check retained for historical comparison; it is not primary MLIR performance evidence.

paper_use.decision: Retain as a native C++ sanity check; do not use for the MLIR method's primary performance claim.
usable_claims: Early native tiling variants were executable and measurable.

entries.title: Historical QEMU Scalar and RVV Correctness Check
entries.claim_boundary: Scalar and RVV emulator correctness evidence only; no real-board performance conclusion is supported.

paper_use.decision: Correctness-only emulator evidence is outside the current x86 BLIS thesis evaluation.
usable_claims: Scalar and RVV test executables passed the recorded QEMU correctness check.

entries.title: Historical RVV Toolchain and Instruction-generation Check
entries.claim_boundary: RVV toolchain and instruction-generation evidence only; it does not establish emulator correctness or real-board performance.

paper_use.decision: Toolchain evidence only; it does not support runtime performance conclusions.
usable_claims: The configured toolchain emitted recorded RVV instructions.

entries.title: Historical Transform Dialect to RVV Code-generation Demo
entries.claim_boundary: Transform and RVV code-generation evidence; fixed-size MLIR vectors and generated RVV instructions do not by themselves establish target-hardware speedup.

paper_use.decision: Keep as code-generation evidence; fixed vectors and RVV instructions do not establish target speedup.
usable_claims: Transform Dialect generated the recorded RVV-oriented IR and object code.

entries.title: CGO 2025 Transform Dialect Section 4.5 Core Materials
entries.claim_boundary: The extracted files document the Section 4.5 tuner interface and reusable patterns, but missing support files prevent claiming a standalone local reproduction or observed library replacement.

paper_use.decision: Use as a source-grounded interface reference, not as a local reproduction or local measurement.
usable_claims: The extracted Section 4.5 materials expose a reusable parameterized Transform and tuner interface pattern.

entries.title: CGO-strengthening f64, True-online, Optuna and Multi-source Evaluation
entries.claim_boundary: Single-thread local x86 evidence for f32/f64 BLIS Haswell Contracts, fresh-process online tuning, Optuna TPE and source-labelled soft-prior ranking. It does not establish cross-host or cross-architecture generalization.

recommended_sections: Chapter 6 additional dtype and live tuning evaluation
paper_use.decision: Primary extension evidence for f64 Contract transfer, genuine online measurement, an equal-budget Optuna TPE baseline, and source-labelled BLIS/OpenBLAS prior ablation. Use the low-budget gains over random and naive source merging; do not claim statistically significant superiority over Optuna or cross-host generalization.
usable_claims: The f64 Haswell dgemm 6x8 path passed 3132 trials over 36 shapes, with all direct/adapt predictions matching runtime observations.
At budget 5, offline and online calibrated policies were 1.192x and 1.155x faster than random in paired geometric comparisons, with both 95% bootstrap intervals above one.
Online calibration did not significantly outperform Optuna TPE at budget 5 or 25; all 7500 tuner objectives were fresh subprocess measurements with zero objective-cache hits.
BLIS-only and calibrated source priors both outperformed naive BLIS/OpenBLAS merging at budget 5, while calibrated and BLIS-only performance were statistically indistinguishable.

entries.title: Chapter 3 BLIS and OpenBLAS Multi-source Expert Facts
entries.claim_boundary: Pinned BLIS/OpenBLAS source extraction and four-shape host OpenBLAS CBLAS validation. Facts remain backend-specific: hard compatibility contracts may transfer conditionally, while blocking values are soft implementation hints rather than universal performance laws.

paper_use.decision: Primary evidence that hard compatibility knowledge and soft performance hints are separated across BLIS and OpenBLAS. Host OpenBLAS runtime validation supports extraction fidelity; backend-specific conflicts must not be collapsed into universal constants.
usable_claims: BLIS and OpenBLAS expose source-linked but backend-specific microkernel, packing, layout and blocking facts.
Cross-source conflicts motivate hard compatibility contracts plus calibratable soft performance priors.
The pinned OpenBLAS Haswell CBLAS probe passed all four shapes and 28 real trials.

entries.title: Chapter 3 GEMM Expert Knowledge and Preliminary MLIR Evidence
entries.claim_boundary: Automated artifacts, the preliminary MLIR AOT path, and all 16 source-backed thesis-author signoffs are complete. All correct Transform candidates were still observed fallback rather than BLIS replacement.

paper_use.decision: Primary evidence for Fact/Contract/Rule modeling and the eight-shape preliminary MLIR study. All 16 source-backed review cases have explicit thesis-author signoff.
usable_claims: The extractor produced source-linked facts and contracts with explicit evidence and review state.
The eight-shape preliminary MLIR/AOT dataset quantifies legal-space reduction, fallback, correctness, and compile cost.

entries.title: Chapter 4 Candidate Space to Chapter 5 BLIS Evaluator Integration
entries.claim_boundary: A six-evaluation integration smoke test on one x86-64 AVX2/FMA host. It proves that Chapter 4 Expert+Shell candidates and unmodified BaCO callbacks drive the Chapter 5 real BLIS direct/adapt/fallback evaluator, but it is not the formal multi-space, multi-seed Chapter 6 comparison.

paper_use.decision: Primary integration evidence that Chapter 4 candidates and BaCO callbacks now drive observed Chapter 5 direct/adapt/fallback execution. The six-point tuning data is not a formal convergence comparison.
usable_claims: Checker-predicted direct, adapt, and fallback cases match observed runtime paths.
The integrated BaCO callback reaches real BLIS microkernel evaluations with candidate-level correctness and path evidence.

entries.title: Chapter 4 Microkernel-aware Schedule-space Experiment Package
entries.claim_boundary: The method and tuner loop are complete. Predicted direct/adapt paths remain observed fallback because no real BLIS packing or library-call adapter is present.

paper_use.decision: Primary method evidence for B1-B4 space construction, Checker decisions, provenance, complexity, and BaCO interface. Its old path examples remain observed fallback.
usable_claims: The implementation generates Generic, Contract-only, Expert-core, and Expert+Shell spaces with explicit provenance.
The six-task filtering funnel and generation-overhead measurements are reproducible.
Unmodified BaCO consumed the exported finite lookup in a bounded smoke run.

entries.title: Chapter 5 Real BLIS Packing and Microkernel Call Adapter
entries.claim_boundary: Rank-2 row-major f32 static direct, M/N/K fringe adapt, and one-binary dynamic-shape execution now perform real BLIS-compatible packing and call bli_sgemm_haswell_asm_6x16. Multi-shape speedups use a scalar-loop dynamic fallback; arbitrary layouts, additional dtypes/contracts, optimized dynamic baselines, parallel execution, and cross-host statistics remain outside this evidence.

paper_use.decision: Primary evidence for real packing, BLIS 6x16 invocation, fringe adaptation, and dynamic shape correctness. Performance remains preliminary because the dynamic baseline is scalar-loop.
usable_claims: The generated binary contains and executes the real BLIS Haswell f32 6x16 microkernel path.
Seven dynamic shapes validate direct/adapt execution, nonzero-C accumulation, and runtime fringe counts.
Reported speedups are only relative to the declared scalar-loop dynamic fallback.

entries.title: Chapter 6 Qwen Trace and K230 MLIR Application Validation
entries.claim_boundary: The real Qwen trace, physical MLIR-to-RV64GCV wrapper closure, physical K230 pool and native i9-14900 exact-shape pool are complete. Trace weighting projects measured GEMM times over the frozen call trace and is not end-to-end model latency. The i7 WSL pool remains diagnostic-only; no hardware run remains pending inside the frozen thesis boundary.

recommended_sections: Chapter 6 application trace and deployment closure
paper_use.decision: Supporting evidence for model-code workload relevance, physical-K230 MLIR-to-RV64GCV library-adapter closure, and trace-weighted projection over completed K230 and i9-14900 exact-shape pools. The local WSL timing pool remains excluded, and all weighted values are measured-GEMM projections rather than end-to-end model latency.
usable_claims: Executing the Hugging Face Qwen2ForCausalLM control flow with stateless shape-preserving modules records 338 raw Linear calls, 336 core projection calls and 192 canonical fused projection calls across two frozen scenarios.
The eight frozen Qwen workload rows cover 100 percent of the 24-block core projection calls and FLOPs represented by the canonical trace; lm_head is explicitly excluded.
All 24 per-workload tile-parameter audits are accounted for: 20 are active and four decode tile_m dimensions are conditionally eliminated by static legality.

Three predeclared direct/fringe cases lower from linalg and Transform parameters to LLVM IR, link the existing OpenBLAS packing/sgemm_kernel adapter into a static RV64GCV ELF, and pass both the host ABI smoke and a 45-row physical K230 correctness/path run without modifying the frozen Chapter 4 generator or adding a compiler pass.

On the physical K230, the C-adapter/MLIR-adapter geometric time ratio is 0.9887 with a cluster-bootstrap 95 percent interval of [0.9765, 1.0009], satisfying the preregistered wrapper-parity criterion.
The eight exact Qwen shapes complete their physical K230 candidate pools; the best generated adapter reaches 0.6653 geometric relative performance versus complete OpenBLAS on those shapes.

For both frozen trace scenarios, a library guard restores projected GEMM latency to the complete-OpenBLAS baseline, but no generated candidate beats the library and the one-time tuning cost therefore has no positive break-even point.
The 2088-process i7 confirmation and 120-process WSL qualification are retained as negative measurement-quality evidence; neither supports a local performance or search-benefit claim.
The trace-weighted analysis consumes both the completed native i9-14900 and physical K230 exact-shape pools; no hardware run remains pending within the frozen thesis boundary.

entries.title: Chapter 6 Contract and Schedule-space Validation
entries.claim_boundary: Static and replay-based validation over three frozen Contract packages. It supports mutation safety, callable-density and bounded reuse/economic claims; it does not turn manifest replay into fresh compilation, prove universal zero-core-change onboarding, or claim that generated schedules outperform complete BLIS/OpenBLAS.

recommended_sections: Chapter 4 compatibility checker and schedule-space construction
paper_use.decision: Primary audit evidence for Contract mutation safety, canonicalized intrinsic schedule-space statistics, quantified schema reuse boundaries and horizon-aware search economics. Use the positive safety/density results together with the pre-normalization performance-metric boundary, partial cross-library generator reuse and complete-library no-break-even results.
usable_claims: Across three independently frozen package manifests, all 24 dangerous single-field mutations were rejected or safely downgraded, with zero false accepts and zero false rejects over 30 gold cases.

Across 36 workloads and 2600 raw candidates per workload, canonicalizing inactive vectorize on direct/adapt microkernel paths reduced the mean Contract-only set from 208.7 to 104.4 unique schedules, with callable density 1.000 and zero residual normalized-IR duplication in C.

The post-canonicalization candidate, path, density, IR-hash and first-callable metrics are offline recomputations; D90 and time-to-90% remain pre-normalization historical measurements because the tuner was not rerun.

The three package schemas have 31 intersection and 34 union leaf paths (IoU 0.912), but the OpenBLAS RVV package currently uses a package-local generation path; universal zero-core-change onboarding is not established.

A finite positive break-even exists only for an explicitly post-hoc i7 upper-bound scenario: point and LCB95 break-even are 165865 and 741587 synthetic deployment cycles; the frozen i9 and K230 exact traces have negative savings denominators against complete BLIS/OpenBLAS.

entries.title: Chapter 6 Formal Kernel-aware GEMM Evaluation
entries.claim_boundary: Formal B1-B4 equal-budget, five-seed evaluation over 36 predeclared row-major f32 shapes on one single-thread x86-64 AVX2/FMA host, with optimized dynamic MLIR and complete BLIS references, ablations, shape holdout, and overhead. BaCO uses offline replay over real measured candidates. Confidence intervals do not represent cross-host variation; additional dtype/architecture performance remains outside this completed run. Chapter 3 author signoff is complete and separately recorded.

paper_use.decision: Primary formal evidence for RQ1-RQ6 within the single-host row-major f32 boundary. Use its B1-B4 comparison, complete-BLIS reference, ablations, holdout, overhead, and confidence intervals; do not extend the result to additional dtypes or hosts.
usable_claims: Thirty-six predeclared shapes and eight core tuning shapes provide the formal space-quality and baseline matrix.

B1-B4 use equal 25-candidate budgets, five seeds, one real measurement corpus, and pinned BaCO 3.0; finite domains, seeds, and objective values are unchanged, while runtime compatibility interventions are disclosed separately.
The evaluation includes a genuinely vectorized dynamic MLIR reference, complete BLIS bli_sgemm, component ablations, shell sensitivity, zero-shot holdout, and system overhead.

entries.title: Chapter 6 i9 and K230 Frozen External Validation Packages
entries.claim_boundary: The native i9 and physical K230 ten-workload pools are complete. The i9 run passes 870 correctness rows and reaches 0.5448 geometric relative performance versus complete BLIS with 95% CI [0.4833,0.6104]; the K230 run passes 429 rows and reaches 0.6730 versus complete OpenBLAS with 95% CI [0.6193,0.7253]. Both generated pools win 0/10 workloads. This supports cross-host legality, independent-backend extensibility and negative complete-library performance boundaries, not end-to-end model latency or universal x86/RISC-V generalization.

recommended_sections: Chapter 6 independent-backend extensibility and target-library boundary
Appendix reproducibility and external validation protocol
paper_use.decision: The physical K230 and native i9-14900 exact-shape pools are complete. Use them as claim-eligible supporting evidence for independent OpenBLAS/RV64GCV backend integration, cross-host Contract legality, and negative complete-library performance boundaries; do not claim generated schedules outperform the complete libraries.
usable_claims: The i9 and K230 measurement protocols and immutable deployment archives were frozen before external execution.
The OpenBLAS RV64GCV extension preserves the frozen core generator hash and adds a Contract, candidate data, explicit packing/call adapter, and deployment code.
The static K230 ELF contains the real cblas_sgemm and sgemm_kernel symbols, and 429 of 429 physical-board rows pass strict candidate-coverage, path and correctness checks over ten workloads.
The per-workload pool oracle selects eight direct and two adapt schedules, demonstrating automatic legal scheduling and fringe handling without changing the frozen core generator.

The best generated adapter reaches 0.6730 geometric relative performance versus complete OpenBLAS with a workload-bootstrap 95 percent interval of [0.6193, 0.7253] and wins zero of ten workloads; this is a measured negative performance boundary rather than a library-speedup claim.
Across 143 timing groups, median trial CV is 0.158 percent; the maximum CV among selected best adapters is 0.300 percent and maximum numerical projection error is 5.99e-6.
The native i9-14900 import passes archive integrity, executable and manifest verification, candidate completeness, path agreement and correctness checks for 870 rows over ten workloads.

On the i9-14900, the best generated Contract pool reaches 0.5448 geometric relative performance versus complete BLIS with workload-bootstrap 95 percent interval [0.4833, 0.6104] and wins zero of ten workloads.
The i9 sparse Data-only versus Expert+Data ablation finds zero significant benefits and two significant harms, so the incremental BLIS-derived soft performance-feature claim remains stopped.

entries.title: Chapter 6 Online-calibrated Soft Prior Evaluation
entries.claim_boundary: A same-host f32 study over a 1008-case real measurement corpus. It evaluates static, offline-calibrated, online-calibrated and misleading priors under fixed replay budgets; it does not yet establish cross-host or cross-architecture transfer.

paper_use.decision: Primary evidence for separating static source knowledge from target-calibrated soft priors. The same-host holdout shows strong offline calibration and no additional early-budget gain from online updating once the offline model is already trained; the misleading-prior control demonstrates recovery rather than guaranteed dominance.
usable_claims: A 1008-case real measurement corpus supports design, validation and held-out online-prior comparisons without synthetic objective values.
Static BLIS ranking is weakly correlated with target performance, while calibrated ranking is substantially stronger on all splits.
Online feedback can recover from a deliberately misleading prior, but does not automatically outperform a well-trained offline prior on the same host.

entries.title: Chapter 6 Strict Same-pool Calibrated-prior Evaluation
entries.claim_boundary: A formal single-thread replay over complete i7-10750H and i9-14900 f64 Contract corpora. It establishes strict same-pool controls, liveness, unique-budget and leakage safeguards, and shared-oracle invariance. Calibrated-prior gains are significant on i7 but inconclusive on i9, so the planned two-environment replication gate fails. It makes no K230 or non-x86 prior-transfer claim.

paper_use.decision: Primary same-pool evidence that repairs the B2/B3/B4 comparison with a canonical common pool, active-parameter audit, unique-measurement budgets, holdout-safe calibration, and a shared oracle. The i7 result supports local calibrated-prior gains at budgets 5 and 10, while the completed i9 replication is inconclusive; the preregistered two-environment acceptance gate therefore fails.
usable_claims: The canonical pool contains 23-26 unique active schedules per workload after removing the inactive vectorize alias; tile_m, tile_n, and tile_k pass measured liveness checks.
B2, B3, and B4 replay the same complete candidate pool with no-replacement budgets, zero duplicate objective callbacks, no holdout target reads, and an invariant common-pool oracle.
On the i7-10750H holdout, calibrated B4 improves over no-prior B2 by 1.058x at budget 5 and 1.035x at budget 10, with workload-cluster bootstrap intervals wholly above one.
The static B3 prior is inconclusive against B2 at both checkpoints, separating source-derived ranking from target-calibrated performance knowledge.

On the i9-14900 holdout, calibrated B4 versus no-prior B2 is 0.992x at budget 5 and 0.999x at budget 10, with both confidence intervals crossing one; the requested two-environment significance condition is not satisfied.

entries.title: Chapter 6 Prior and Extensibility Termination Validation
entries.claim_boundary: A single-host i7-10750H f64 same-pool termination study. It establishes target-data low-budget gains, rejects demonstrated incremental value for the current BLIS-derived soft performance features, measures non-amortization over 12 holdouts, and validates a second BLIS Haswell microkernel Contract with the unchanged core generator. It does not establish an independent backend or broad practical calibration benefit.

recommended_sections: Chapter 6 termination validation and calibration economics
paper_use.decision: Primary same-pool termination evidence separating target-data calibration from BLIS-derived soft performance features. Use the significant Data-only gain over no-prior, the absence of a significant Expert+Data increment, the measured non-amortization boundary, and the second f64 Contract extension. Do not call the f64 kernel an independent backend.
usable_claims: Under identical pools, seeds and unique budgets, Data-only improves over no-prior BaCO by 1.049x at budget 5 and 1.035x at budget 10, with workload-cluster confidence intervals above one.
Expert+Data does not significantly improve over Data-only: the budget-5 interval crosses one and the budget-10 result is effectively identical.
The calibrated methods reduce mean time-to-95%-oracle from 7.90 to 3.08 candidate evaluations, but the 591-row calibration cost does not amortize within the 12 frozen holdout workloads.

The unchanged core space generator selects the f64 6x8 Contract for all 36 workloads, creates direct/adapt schedules, and passes fresh direct/fringe runtime checks plus full-pool search without duplicate objectives.
The extension result establishes a second BLIS Haswell microkernel Contract, not an independent library, ISA, or backend.

entries.title: K230 C908 RVV and OpenBLAS Deployment ELF Package
entries.claim_boundary: Physical K230 C908 RT-Smart results are available for the recorded scalar, explicit-RVV and complete OpenBLAS binaries across eight workloads. Claims are limited to this board, toolchain, runtime and harness; they do not establish universal RISC-V performance generalization.

recommended_sections: Discussion: transferable compatibility knowledge and calibrated performance priors
paper_use.decision: Physical K230 RT-Smart measurements are available for scalar, explicit-RVV and full OpenBLAS paths across eight workloads. Use as supporting cross-architecture evidence under the recorded board configuration.
usable_claims: The physical K230 run produced 560 correct timing rows across eight workloads, including non-divisible fringe shapes.
Selecting the best measured explicit-RVV candidate per workload achieved a 2.29x geometric-mean speedup over its in-process scalar control.
The complete OpenBLAS RISCV64_ZVL128B SGEMM path achieved a 13.47x geometric-mean speedup over the in-process scalar control.

No fixed source-derived schedule dominated all workloads; generic, OpenBLAS-derived and cross-source candidates won different shapes, while the BLIS-derived fixed candidate was not a reliable performance prior.

entries.title: Transformer Fan-out Shared-packing Go/No-go
entries.claim_boundary: A single-host WSL2, single-thread, row-major f32 mechanism probe using Qwen2.5-0.5B QKV and Gate/Up dimensions, complete BLIS paths, persistent packed weights, and the real BLIS Haswell 6x16 microkernel. It rejects shared activation packing as a standalone local mainline under this Contract; it does not evaluate an MLIR graph planner, low-precision kernels, fused epilogues, complete Transformer blocks, or cross-host generalization.

paper_use.decision: Local go/no-go evidence rejects shared activation packing as a standalone main contribution under the measured f32 BLIS Contract. Preserve it as a negative-result boundary; future region planning must obtain value from additional mechanisms such as fusion, heterogeneous layouts/dtypes, or phase-specific kernel portfolios.
usable_claims: All 160 formal rows across QKV and Gate/Up fan-out regions passed correctness with real BLIS complete calls and the real Haswell 6x16 microkernel.
Shared activation packing was 1.006x relative to the otherwise identical repacked-A microkernel path, with a 95% case-bootstrap interval spanning one.
Shared activation packing reached only 0.720x relative to concatenated complete BLIS and 0.665x relative to the faster complete-BLIS path selected per paired trial.
Measured activation-pack savings imply a packing-only ideal speedup of 1.010x geometric mean and at most 1.032x, below the frozen 1.05x go threshold.

entries.title: Intel Core i9-14900 Cross-host Evaluation
entries.claim_boundary: A two-host, single-thread row-major x86-64 comparison using the same fixed revisions, f64 BLIS Haswell 6x8 Contract, candidate domains and tuner budgets. It supports hard compatibility transfer and bounded low-budget sample-efficiency claims, but not universal x86 ranking, multithreaded behavior or asymptotic tuner superiority.

paper_use.decision: Supporting two-host x86 evidence under the same fixed f64 Contract, candidate domains and tuner budgets. Use the replicated compatibility checks, low-budget gains and host-dependent ranking reversal; do not claim universal x86 generalization or budget-25 tuner dominance.
usable_claims: The returned Intel Core i9-14900 archive passed SHA-256, revision, CPU-family and formal-status validation.

Both x86 hosts completed 3132 f64 Contract trials with complete direct/adapt prediction agreement, 6000 fresh true-online subprocess measurements with zero objective-cache hits, and 1500 unique Optuna measurements.

At budget 5, offline and online calibrated policies were both about 1.060x faster than random on the i9-14900 with 95% intervals above one; online calibration was 1.065x faster than Optuna TPE with interval [1.044, 1.089].
The generated f64 candidate pool changed from 1.064x relative to complete BLIS on the development host to 0.804x on the i9-14900, showing that absolute performance ranking is not host invariant.
At budget 25 the online policy did not consistently dominate random or Optuna, so the supported benefit is low-budget guidance rather than asymptotic superiority.
